title: Lavatera maroccana
intro: Lavatera maroccana, also known as Moroccan hibiscus or rose mallow, is a flowering plant in the Malvaceae family, characterized by its large, showy flowers and edible calyces. The plant is rich in various health benefits, including its ability to help lower blood pressure, reduce inflammation, and exhibit antioxidant properties. Its bioactive constituents include flavonoids, anthocyanins, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The plant is commonly used to make herbal teas, infusions, and decoctions, often used to support cardiovascular health and as a natural remedy for various digestive issues.
taxonomy: Lavatera maroccana is classified as a species within the genus Lavatera, which is part of the family Malvaceae. The Malvaceae family consists of over 200 genera and includes many species of mallow and hibiscus. This classification is based on the plant's physical characteristics, such as its flowers and leaves. The Lavatera genus is native to the Mediterranean region and includes several species of shrub-like plants. Lavatera maroccana is a specific species within this genus, known for its unique characteristics and growth habits.
uses: Lavatera maroccana, also known as Moroccan hibiscus or rose mallow, is a plant that has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. Its flowers, leaves, and roots are rich in nutrients and have been used to treat different health conditions. The plant is said to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help in reducing inflammation and protecting against cell damage. The plant's active constituents have been studied for their antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities. side_effects: Lavatera maroccana, also known as the Moroccan hibiscus or rose mallow, is a flowering plant species native to North Africa and the Mediterranean region. While the plant itself is generally not toxic, its seeds and leaves may cause gastrointestinal upset in some individuals if ingested. The plant's sap may also cause skin irritation in people with sensitive skin. However, there is limited information available on the specific side effects of Lavatera maroccana, and more research is needed to fully understand its potential impacts on human health.
